cluster.source_basis: exercise_card_details_for_llm.md Card v2 authoring guide; Purpose.txt not found in available File Library searches
cluster.scoring_rule: Strength and body control are prioritized. Explosiveness is not used as a primary score; resistance work should not be turned into plyometric or conditioning work unless re-authored for another phase.

cards.name: Tempo Bodyweight Squat
cards.cardSummary: Slow-tempo squat that builds leg strength, range ownership, and position control without external load. Use it when the goal is force production, tissue capacity, and repeatable positions with bands or body resistance rather than machines, free weights, or conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare & Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with enough rest that band tension, body position, and rep quality stay strength-focused.
cards.description: Perform Tempo Bodyweight Squat as controlled resistance work. The athlete uses bodyweight leverage to create a strength or control demand while keeping pain-free range, posture, and tempo repeatable.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: squat / knee / dominant / strength. Score Strength and body-control transfer honestly. Use band tension, leverage, tempo, range, and rest to keep Tempo Bodyweight Squat as resistance work. Do not turn it into a rushed conditioning station or over-tag it as explosiveness simply because the athlete is working hard.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, control the hard range, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: It lets coaches make a basic squat strength-focused by adding tempo, pauses, and strict depth quality. Band and body-resistance training works when the athlete controls leverage, tempo, and alignment instead of relying on momentum. This creates usable force, tissue tolerance, and position ownership with low impact and minimal equipment.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is progressive resistance, force production, and tissue capacity.
cards.commonMisuse: The common mistake is chasing reps, band tension, or speed after position quality drops. This should build strength and tissue capacity, not become a burnout circuit before skill, speed, or power work.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by leverage, support, range of motion, band thickness, tempo, hold duration, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep pain-free range, clean alignment, steady breathing, and repeatable reps.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tempo Bodyweight Squat uses bodyweight leverage to train slow-tempo squat that builds leg strength, range ownership, and position control without external load.
setup: Have the athlete stand with feet about shoulder-width and ribs stacked over pelvis.
Choose a range and resistance level the athlete can own without pain.
Set the brace before the first rep and rehearse the finish position.
execution_steps: Begin from the organized start position for Tempo Bodyweight Squat.
Lower for a controlled three-count, pause briefly in the owned bottom range, then stand without bouncing.
Move with controlled tempo through the full range the athlete can own.
Reset posture and breath before the next repetition or side.
coach_cues: Slow where it matters, strong through the finish.
Final rep should look like the first.
breathing_cues: Exhale through the finish or during the hold without losing shape.
common_faults: Chasing more reps or resistance after alignment changes.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing through the hard range.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Changing stance, band path, or trunk position to survive the set.
quality_gate: No pain.
Start and finish positions match.
Band path or body line stays repeatable.
Final rep or final five seconds stays clean.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Band anchor slips, grip fails, or support surface moves.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the easiest version that preserves alignment, breathing, and repeatable range. Use light assistance, shorter range, slow coaching, and low set volume. Scale body angle, box height, range, tempo, support, and pauses before adding total volume.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate leverage or light-to-moderate band tension for 2-3 clean sets. Progress only when the athlete can explain and repeat the key cues. Scale body angle, box height, range, tempo, support, and pauses before adding total volume.
scaling.teen: Progress range, band tension, leverage, or tempo with 1-3 reps in reserve. Keep enough rest that this stays strength/control work, not conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start conservative with support, reduced range, lighter band tension, or fewer reps. Build consistency before chasing harder variations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with stronger bands, longer lever arms, slower eccentrics, pauses, unilateral demand, or added set density while preserving the intended phase and quality.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, shorter ranges, lighter bands, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use medical guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, high pressure bracing, and positions that create symptoms; use clinician guidance when appropriate.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, leverage, band tension, symptoms, limb length, coordination, and recovery rather than gender.
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning placed later if quality remains high
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears or increases during the movement.
Athlete cannot maintain the required joint alignment or trunk position.
Breathing becomes breath-holding panic instead of controlled bracing.
The session priority is fresh speed, jumping, or skill work and this would create fatigue first.
mediaReferences: Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Card v2 phase, taxonomy, dosage, scaling, and safety rules
mediaInternalNotes: Film Tempo Bodyweight Squat from front/side or anchor-line angle as appropriate.
Show one regression, the standard version, and one progression.
Keep resistance intent clear; do not program as HIIT unless authored as a separate Sustained Capacity card.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded or easiest version without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness or excessive breath holding.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Painful knee, hip, ankle, or back loading in the pattern.

Have the athlete stand on a long band or use a safe low anchor with band tension balanced through the hands or shoulders.
Choose a range and resistance level the athlete can own without pain.

Sit into a controlled squat, keep knees tracking, then stand against increasing band tension.
Move with controlled tempo through the full range the athlete can own.
Reset posture and breath before the next repetition or side.
coach_cues: Control the band; do not let it control you.
Slow where it matters, strong through the finish.
Final rep should look like the first.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Banded Good Morning uses band tension to train band-resisted hip hinge that builds posterior-chain strength with scalable tension.
setup: Inspect the band for wear and set a secure anchor or foot position before the athlete loads it.

Slow where it matters, strong through the finish.

cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare & Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with enough rest that band tension, body position, and rep quality stay strength-focused.
cards.description: Perform Tempo Push-Up as controlled resistance work. The athlete uses bodyweight leverage to create a strength or control demand while keeping pain-free range, posture, and tempo repeatable.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: upper / body / push / strength. Score Strength and body-control transfer honestly. Use band tension, leverage, tempo, range, and rest to keep Tempo Push-Up as resistance work. Do not turn it into a rushed conditioning station or over-tag it as explosiveness simply because the athlete is working hard.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, control the hard range, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: Tempo makes a normal push-up strength-focused and exposes whether the athlete owns trunk and shoulder positions. Band and body-resistance training works when the athlete controls leverage, tempo, and alignment instead of relying on momentum. This creates usable force, tissue tolerance, and position ownership with low impact and minimal equipment.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is progressive resistance, force production, and tissue capacity.
cards.commonMisuse: The common mistake is chasing reps, band tension, or speed after position quality drops. This should build strength and tissue capacity, not become a burnout circuit before skill, speed, or power work.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by leverage, support, range of motion, band thickness, tempo, hold duration, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep pain-free range, clean alignment, steady breathing, and repeatable reps.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tempo Push-Up uses bodyweight leverage to train slow-lowering push-up that increases pressing strength and eccentric control without adding load.
setup: Have the athlete set the same plank alignment as a regular push-up and choose a range that can be controlled.
Choose a range and resistance level the athlete can own without pain.
Set the brace before the first rep and rehearse the finish position.
execution_steps: Begin from the organized start position for Tempo Push-Up.
Lower for a three-count, pause briefly, and press up without sagging or flaring.
Move with controlled tempo through the full range the athlete can own.
Reset posture and breath before the next repetition or side.
coach_cues: Slow where it matters, strong through the finish.
Final rep should look like the first.
Push the floor or band away with quiet ribs.
breathing_cues: Exhale through the finish or during the hold without losing shape.
common_faults: Chasing more reps or resistance after alignment changes.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing through the hard range.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Changing stance, band path, or trunk position to survive the set.
quality_gate: No pain.
Start and finish positions match.
Band path or body line stays repeatable.
Final rep or final five seconds stays clean.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Band anchor slips, grip fails, or support surface moves.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the easiest version that preserves alignment, breathing, and repeatable range. Use light assistance, shorter range, slow coaching, and low set volume. Scale body angle, box height, range, tempo, support, and pauses before adding total volume.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate leverage or light-to-moderate band tension for 2-3 clean sets. Progress only when the athlete can explain and repeat the key cues. Scale body angle, box height, range, tempo, support, and pauses before adding total volume.
scaling.teen: Progress range, band tension, leverage, or tempo with 1-3 reps in reserve. Keep enough rest that this stays strength/control work, not conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start conservative with support, reduced range, lighter band tension, or fewer reps. Build consistency before chasing harder variations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with stronger bands, longer lever arms, slower eccentrics, pauses, unilateral demand, or added set density while preserving the intended phase and quality.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, shorter ranges, lighter bands, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use medical guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, high pressure bracing, and positions that create symptoms; use clinician guidance when appropriate.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, leverage, band tension, symptoms, limb length, coordination, and recovery rather than gender.
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning placed later if quality remains high
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears or increases during the movement.
Athlete cannot maintain the required joint alignment or trunk position.
Breathing becomes breath-holding panic instead of controlled bracing.
The session priority is fresh speed, jumping, or skill work and this would create fatigue first.

cards.name: Tall-Kneeling Band Chop
cards.cardSummary: Tall-kneeling diagonal band chop that builds controlled rotation, anti-rotation braking, and trunk-hip linkage. Use it when the goal is position ownership, joint control, and tissue durability under low-to-moderate resistance with bands or body resistance rather than machines, free weights, or conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Resilience phase after main Capacity work, or earlier only when the dose is low and the goal is activation/control rather than fatigue. Stop the set when bracing, alignment, or breathing quality drops.
cards.description: Perform Tall-Kneeling Band Chop as controlled resistance work. The athlete uses band tension to create a strength or control demand while keeping pain-free range, posture, and tempo repeatable.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: trunk / pelvis / anti / movement / control. Score Strength and body-control transfer honestly. Use band tension, leverage, tempo, range, and rest to keep Tall-Kneeling Band Chop as resistance work. Do not turn it into a rushed conditioning station or over-tag it as explosiveness simply because the athlete is working hard.
cards.athleteLanguage: Brace, breathe, and own the position. The rep ends when the shape changes.
cards.whyItWorks: It trains rotation as a controlled strength quality rather than a fast throw or sloppy twisting drill. Band and body-resistance training works when the athlete controls leverage, tempo, and alignment instead of relying on momentum. This creates usable force, tissue tolerance, and position ownership with low impact and minimal equipment.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience because the main adaptation is control, bracing, joint ownership, and tissue durability under manageable resistance.
cards.commonMisuse: The common mistake is turning a control drill into a long, shaky endurance test. Use enough resistance to challenge position, but stop before breath-holding, compensation, or joint discomfort becomes the training effect.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by leverage, support, range of motion, band thickness, tempo, hold duration, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep pain-free range, clean alignment, steady breathing, and repeatable reps.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Tall-Kneeling Band Chop uses band tension to train tall-kneeling diagonal band chop that builds controlled rotation, anti-rotation braking, and trunk-hip linkage.
setup: Inspect the band for wear and set a secure anchor or foot position before the athlete loads it.
Have the athlete anchor a band high and kneel tall sideways or slightly angled to the anchor.
Choose a range and resistance level the athlete can own without pain.
Set the brace before the first rep and rehearse the finish position.
execution_steps: Begin from the organized start position for Tall-Kneeling Band Chop.
Pull the band diagonally across the body, rotate only through owned range, then return slowly.
Move with controlled tempo through the full range the athlete can own.
Reset posture and breath before the next repetition or side.
coach_cues: Control the band; do not let it control you.
Slow where it matters, strong through the finish.
Final rep should look like the first.
Pull elbows to the target without shrugging.
breathing_cues: Exhale through the finish or during the hold without losing shape.
common_faults: Chasing more reps or resistance after alignment changes.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing through the hard range.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Changing stance, band path, or trunk position to survive the set.
Using a band that is too heavy or anchored at the wrong angle.
quality_gate: No pain.
Start and finish positions match.
Band path or body line stays repeatable.
Final rep or final five seconds stays clean.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Band anchor slips, grip fails, or support surface moves.
Breathing becomes dizziness, panic bracing, or uncontrolled breath holding.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the easiest version that preserves alignment, breathing, and repeatable range. Use light assistance, shorter range, slow coaching, and low set volume. Scale band thickness, anchor distance, and band angle before adding reps.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate leverage or light-to-moderate band tension for 2-3 clean sets. Progress only when the athlete can explain and repeat the key cues. Scale band thickness, anchor distance, and band angle before adding reps.
scaling.teen: Progress range, band tension, leverage, or tempo with 1-3 reps in reserve. Keep enough rest that this stays strength/control work, not conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start conservative with support, reduced range, lighter band tension, or fewer reps. Build consistency before chasing harder variations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with stronger bands, longer lever arms, slower eccentrics, pauses, unilateral demand, or added set density while preserving the intended phase and quality.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, shorter ranges, lighter bands, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use medical guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, high pressure bracing, and positions that create symptoms; use clinician guidance when appropriate.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, leverage, band tension, symptoms, limb length, coordination, and recovery rather than gender.
pairsWellAfter: Main strength work when used as activation only
pairsWellBefore: Main strength work if used as activation
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears or increases during the movement.
Athlete cannot maintain the required joint alignment or trunk position.
Breathing becomes breath-holding panic instead of controlled bracing.
The session priority is fresh speed, jumping, or skill work and this would create fatigue first.
Band anchor, band condition, or band path cannot be made safe.
